summary: Gruber argues that Apple's announcement of a native iPhone SDK is not bad news for iPhone web apps, just as Cocoa wasn't bad news for desktop web apps. He contends that web apps and native apps don't truly compete head-to-head, and that MobileSafari-optimized web development will serve as the 'Visual Basic' of the iPhone platform — easier, more approachable, and accessible to far more developers. He envisions Apple eventually embracing network-based apps as first-class citizens, possibly through a next-generation runtime that out-classes Flash, but acknowledges that day hasn't arrived yet. He closes with a prescient (and ultimately wrong) prediction that the iPod brand will outlast the iPhone brand.
key_points: Web apps and native apps serve fundamentally different purposes and don't compete head-to-head — most good app ideas only make sense one way or the other
MobileSafari web development will be the 'Visual Basic' of the iPhone platform: easier, more approachable, and open to far more developers than native UIKit
Apple will eventually embrace network-based apps as first-class citizens, possibly through a next-generation runtime or simply through WebKit advances and Moore's Law
Jobs's WWDC claim that web apps were a 'sweet' SDK was undermined by the fact that Apple itself didn't use web technologies for any of the iPhone's built-in apps
Gruber predicts the iPod brand will outlast the iPhone brand once ubiquitous wireless IP networking makes carrier-tied phones obsolete
money_quotes: The weird thing about web apps vs. desktop apps is that they don't really compete head-to-head. Most good ideas for apps only make sense one way or the other.
I see MobileSafari-optimized web development as the Visual Basic for the iPhone as a platform. Easier, more approachable, and wide open to everyone.
Apple, someday, is going to be all over 'web apps' as first-class citizens for its computing platforms.
It was never Apple's intention for MobileSafari web apps, here and now, to be as good as native iPhone UIKit apps.
If it were so sweet, then why wasn't Apple using it for the iPhone's built-in apps?
Let the web be the web and let native apps do what native apps do best — the iPhone does both better than any competing platform.
Or maybe just give WebKit and Moore's Law a few more years, and it really will be just HTML/CSS/JavaScript.
key_insight: Web apps and native apps serve complementary rather than competing roles on the iPhone, and the platform's strength lies in excelling at both rather than forcing one to replace the other.
